text:
# Page 1^page=1
Clinical Care Guide: COPD Stability & Biologic Therapy Initiation Patient: Lisa English Date: April 10, 2026 Primary Condition: COPD (Chronic Obstructive Pulmonary Disease) Caregiver Note: This document summarizes current symptom ﬂuctuations and provides a protocol for starting Dupixent (biologic) therapy at home. I. Situation Overview: Understanding "Stacked Irritants" Sometimes COPD gets worse due to surroundings rather than infection. We call this a "stacked irritant day." It means several small things are making it hard to breathe at once. Contributing Factors for Today ● Steroid Taper Rebound: Currently on a 10-day tapering course (3/day). As steroids decrease, underlying inﬂammation can temporarily "bounce back," making airways feel tighter. ● Respiratory Fatigue: Interrupted sleep prevents respiratory muscles from recovering and allows mucus to pool overnight, leading to morning shortness of breath. ● Particulate Irritants: Exposure to incense smoke and air fryer smoke (which triggered a smoke detector) can cause immediate bronchospasm (airway tightening) and inﬂammation in sensitive lungs. ● Mucus Management: Missing doses of cough/decongestion support (Robitussin DM/Equate) can lead to thicker secretions and increased chest discomfort.

# Page 2^page=2
II. The Physics of Breathing: "Deﬂating" the Lungs COPD makes it hard to push air out. Your lungs feel full of old air, so you feel like you can't take a new breath. Method How it Works Purpose Talking Long vocalization requires a slow, controlled exhale. Naturally "deﬂates" the lungs and prevents breath stacking. Pursed-Lip Breathing Exhaling through "straw-like" lips creates back-pressure. Keeps small airways open longer to let more air escape. Abdominal Breathing Uses the diaphragm rather than neck/shoulder muscles. Reduces the eﬀort of breathing and lowers anxiety. Tripod Position Leaning forward with elbows on knees/table. Physically optimizes the diaphragm's ability to move. III. Dupixent (Dupilumab) Initiation Protocol Dupixent is a biologic medication that targets Type 2 inﬂammation. It is not a rescue medication; it is a long-term maintenance therapy designed to reduce ﬂare-ups and dependence on steroids.

# Page 3^page=3
Pre-Injection Checklist 1. Temperature: Remove the pen from the refrigerator 45 minutes prior to injection to reach room temperature. Cold medication stings signiﬁcantly more. 2. Site Selection: Use the front of the thigh or the abdomen (avoiding 2 inches around the navel). 3. Skin Prep: Clean the site with alcohol and let it dry completely. Wet alcohol will cause a burning sensation during the needle entry. 4. Application: Press the pen at a 90-degree angle. Listen for the ﬁrst click (start) and wait for the second click (completion), usually 10 seconds. IV. Caregiver Action Plan & Emergency Card As a caregiver, your role is to observe and follow a structured protocol. Emergency reactions to biologics are rare, obvious, and usually occur within the ﬁrst hour. 🚨 Emergency: Call 911 Immediately ● Swelling: Face, lips, tongue, or throat. ● Severe Distress: Gasping or unable to speak in sentences. ● Blue/Grey Skin: Check lips or ﬁngernails. ● Consciousness: Fainting, severe dizziness, or confusion. ● Hives: Rash spreading quickly. 📞 Urgent: Call the Physician (Same Day) ● Worsening shortness of breath that does not respond to rescue inhalers. ● Fever, chills, or change in mucus color (yellow, green, or brown). ● Severe eye pain or vision changes (a speciﬁc side eﬀect of Dupixent). 🟢 Normal: Monitor & Document ● Mild redness or a small bump at the injection site. ● Temporary fatigue or a mild headache. ● Increased anxiety (which can mimic chest tightness).

# Page 4^page=4
V. Clinician Discussion Points ● Evaluation of current symptoms: Irritant-triggered ﬂare vs. infectious exacerbation. ● Review of maintenance medications (Robitussin DM) and potential interactions with the current steroid taper. ● Long-term goal for Dupixent: When to expect a reduction in steroid frequency. VI. COPD Care Loop: Minimal-Friction Scheduling Protocol This protocol is designed to minimize cognitive load and physical exertion for both the patient and caregivers. By standardizing how appointments and medication tasks are entered into the central system, we eliminate the "rushing" triggers that can lead to respiratory spirals. 1. Appointment Entry Standards Every medical appointment must be entered into the Schedules and Tasks system immediately upon scheduling. To ensure a "no-rush" environment, use the following mandatory data ﬁelds: Field Requirement Detail/Placeholder Primary Driver Who is attending/driving Person Pickup Time Appointment time MINUS 60 mins May 8, 2026 Location Full facility name & address Place Buﬀer Period 30–60 min pre/post block Reserved for "Breathing Prep"

# Page 5^page=5
2. Medication & Pharmacy Integration Reﬁlls and pharmacy tasks should be linked directly to relevant appointments whenever possible to reduce the number of trips. When adding a task related to medications (Maintenance Inhalers, Rescue Inhalers, or Nebulizer supplies), include these details: ● Pharmacy Name: Place ● Reﬁll Status: Note if the reﬁll is ready for pickup or needs a doctor's authorization. ● Oxygen Supply Check: For every outing, verify the portable oxygen tank levels and LPM requirements. 3. The "No-Spiral" Buffer Rule The 30–60 minute buﬀer is a non-negotiable medical necessity. This time is used to: ● Pre-Trip Stabilization: Allow 30 minutes for a "calm start" to prevent shortness of breath (SOB) before leaving the house. ● Post-Trip Recovery: 30–60 minutes of scheduled rest upon returning to monitor O2 saturation and heart rate. ● Traﬃc Contingency: Ensures that unexpected delays do not cause the anxiety that triggers COPD exacerbations. 4. Documentation & References Use the following links to maintain consistency across the care plan: ● Full Care Plan: TBD ● Vitality Tracker: File (Refer to this for daily O2 and BP trends) ● Emergency Contact: Cody Rice-Velasquez 812.266.1369 This framework was developed to address the web of compounding challenges identiﬁed in June 2025 care logs. ● Conﬁrmation of oxygen saturation thresholds and rescue inhaler limits.

# Page 6^page=6
VII. COPD Bedside Care & Vitality Tracker This section provides a structured framework for daily bedside monitoring and a tracker for recording vital signs. Use this to identify trends and provide objective data during clinician discussions. 1. Daily Monitoring Checklist ● Morning Check: Assess O2 saturation and heart rate immediately upon waking. Note any morning chest tightness or excessive mucus. ● Activity Log: Record any signiﬁcant exertion (e.g., showering, walking to the car) and the recovery time required to return to baseline breathing. ● Evening Review: Verify that all maintenance medications were taken and assess the eﬀectiveness of the current steroid taper. 2. Vitality Tracker Table Date/Time O2 Sat (%) Heart Rate Notes (Symptoms/Triggers) May 8, 2026 Note: If O2 saturation falls below the clinician-deﬁned threshold (e.g., 88%) at rest, initiate the emergency protocols outlined in Section IV. VIII. Caregiver Operating Schedule: Working Master Mom Daily Care Schedule — Working Master Time Window Primary Tasks & Focus 07:00 – 08:30 Morning Med Window (Steroids/Maintenance). First O2/HR check. 09:00 – 10:00 Breakfast & First Breathing Treatment (Nebulizer/Inhalers).

# Page 7^page=7
10:00 – 12:00 High Activity Window (Showers/Appointments). Cody Work Block 1. 12:00 – 13:00 Lunch & Post-Activity Recovery Rest. 13:00 – 16:00 Low Activity / Nap Window. Cody Work Block 2. 16:00 – 17:00 Second Breathing Treatment. PM Med Prep. 17:00 – 18:30 Dinner Window. 19:00 – 21:00 Evening Med Window. Final O2 check. CPAP/Bed Prep. Morning Med Window: 07:00 – 08:30 (Focus: Prednisone & Bronchodilators) Evening Med Window: 19:00 – 21:00 (Focus: Sleep Support & Maintenance) Breathing Treatment Rhythm Type Frequency / Triggers Nebulizer Every 4–6 hours as needed; Mandatory Morning/Evening. Rescue Inhaler Max 1 puﬀ every 4 hours for acute SOB. Weekly Help Schedule ● Monday: Physical Therapy (PT) Visit (Morning Window) ● Tuesday: Nurse Check-in / Vitals Review Best Helper Windows & Best Assignments ● Windows: 10:00 – 14:00 (Best for out-of-house tasks or heavy cleaning) ● Assignments: Pharmacy runs, laundry, meal prep, and bedside supervision during Cody work blocks. Cody Protected Time Block Description Morning Work Block 10:00 – 12:00 (Deep Focus Time) Afternoon Work Block 13:30 – 15:30 (Meetings/Emails) Admin Checklist Verify insurance authorizations for Dupixent reﬁlls.

# Page 8^page=8
Update Clinician Discussion Points after every specialist visit. Linen/Hygiene Schedule ● Linens: Change sheets every Wednesday/Saturday (or as needed for hygiene). ● Hygiene: Full shower assist on Tuesday/Friday/Sunday. Meal Rhythm Meal Standard Components Breakfast Protein-heavy, easy to chew (avoiding exertion while eating). Lunch Light soup/sandwich; hydration focus. Dinner Balanced meal; ﬁnished by 18:30 to prevent reﬂux/SOB at bedtime. Weekly Operating Plan Day Core Objective Sunday Med Review & Logistics Prep. Mid-Week Supply Check (Oxygen/Meds). Sunday Pill Organizer / Med Review ● Count current steroid taper doses remaining. ● Reﬁll weekly pill organizer for AM/PM windows. Daily Care Log Template Use the Vitality Tracker in Section VII for O2, HR, and symptoms. Escalation Rules ● Call 911: If you see any emergency markers from Section IV. ● Call Doctor: If O2 stays below 88% or mucus changes color. Clean Version to Put in Calendar (Recurring Tasks) Daily: 07:00 AM Med Window Daily: 19:00 PM Med Window Weekly: Sunday 16:00 Med Review
